story.iWant: To measure comprehension skills aligned with PIRLS international and KS2 national standards
story.soThat: Learning trajectory is tracked using established reading assessment frameworks that educators recognise

assumptions: Student has been guided through a passage or text
Transcript contains student responses to comprehension questions
Tutor has asked open-ended questions about the text

frameworks.pirls: PIRLS 2021 Assessment Framework (IEA/Boston College) — 4 comprehension processes
frameworks.ks2: UK National Curriculum KS2 Reading Content Domains 2a-2h (STA 2016)

acceptanceCriteria.title: Retrieval skill scored
acceptanceCriteria.given: A student locating information in a passage
acceptanceCriteria.when: The system analyses their ability to find and extract explicitly stated facts
acceptanceCriteria.then: A retrieval skill score between 0-1 is produced
acceptanceCriteria.rationale: Retrieval is the foundation of comprehension — can they locate what the text explicitly says? (PIRLS Process 1, KS2 2b)

acceptanceCriteria.title: Inference skill scored
acceptanceCriteria.given: A student answering questions about implied meaning
acceptanceCriteria.when: The system analyses their ability to draw conclusions and justify them with evidence
acceptanceCriteria.then: An inference skill score between 0-1 is produced
acceptanceCriteria.rationale: Inference separates surface reading from comprehension — can they read between the lines and prove it? (PIRLS Process 2, KS2 2d)

acceptanceCriteria.title: Vocabulary in context scored
acceptanceCriteria.given: A student encountering unfamiliar words in a passage
acceptanceCriteria.when: The system analyses their ability to determine word meaning from context
acceptanceCriteria.then: A vocabulary in context score between 0-1 is produced
acceptanceCriteria.rationale: Vocabulary in context shows whether a student can use the text itself to unlock meaning (KS2 2a, cross-cutting PIRLS 1+2)

acceptanceCriteria.title: Language appreciation scored
acceptanceCriteria.given: A student discussing how an author uses language
acceptanceCriteria.when: The system analyses their recognition of how word and phrase choices enhance meaning
acceptanceCriteria.then: A language appreciation score between 0-1 is produced
acceptanceCriteria.rationale: Language appreciation shows critical awareness of authorial craft (PIRLS Process 4, KS2 2g)

acceptanceCriteria.title: Evaluation skill scored
acceptanceCriteria.given: A student interpreting, comparing, or critiquing text content
acceptanceCriteria.when: The system analyses their ability to integrate information and form supported judgements
acceptanceCriteria.then: An evaluation skill score between 0-1 is produced
acceptanceCriteria.rationale: Evaluation shows higher-order thinking — interpreting themes, integrating evidence, forming critical judgements (PIRLS Processes 3+4, KS2 2f+2h)

acceptanceCriteria.title: Recall accuracy scored
acceptanceCriteria.when: The system analyses their recall of content from prior sessions
acceptanceCriteria.then: A recall accuracy score between 0-1 is produced
acceptanceCriteria.rationale: Cross-session retention shows learning consolidation — an HF extension beyond single-sitting assessment frameworks

constraints.description: COMP_RECALL should be scored as null or excluded from aggregation on session 1 (nothing to recall yet)

failureConditions.implication: Not enough student speech to assess comprehension — use neutral defaults

parameters.name: retrieval_skill
parameters.description: Ability to locate and extract explicitly stated information from the text

pirls.name: Focus on and Retrieve Explicitly Stated Information

ks2.name: Retrieve and record information / identify key details from fiction and non-fiction

subMetrics.name: Accuracy
subMetrics.description: Whether the student locates the correct information

definitions.high: Finds the right information quickly and accurately
definitions.low: Cannot locate relevant information, retrieves wrong details

subMetrics.name: Specificity
subMetrics.description: How precisely the student references the text

definitions.high: Quotes specific words, phrases, or identifies exact locations
definitions.low: Vague gestures at the text ('it says somewhere...')

subMetrics.name: Completeness
subMetrics.description: Whether the student retrieves all relevant details, not just one

definitions.high: Identifies multiple relevant details when the text offers them
definitions.low: Finds one detail and stops, missing other relevant information

interpretationScale.label: No Evidence
interpretationScale.implication: Cannot locate information in the text

interpretationScale.label: Emerging
interpretationScale.implication: Locates some information with heavy prompting

interpretationScale.label: Developing
interpretationScale.implication: Finds key details but misses some or is imprecise

interpretationScale.label: Secure
interpretationScale.implication: Accurately retrieves relevant information with specific references

interpretationScale.label: Mastery
interpretationScale.implication: Rapid, precise retrieval of multiple relevant details with exact text references

scoringAnchors.example: I don't know... something about a girl? And there was a place?
scoringAnchors.rationale: Cannot locate any specific information from the text, only vague impressions

scoringAnchors.example: It said she was sent to live with her uncle. I think it was in England somewhere.
scoringAnchors.rationale: Retrieves a key plot point but lacks specificity — no names, no location detail

scoringAnchors.example: Mary was sent to live with her uncle, Mr Craven, at Misselthwaite Manor in Yorkshire after her parents died of cholera in India.
scoringAnchors.rationale: Accurate retrieval with character names and locations, but presented as a single block without distinguishing separate details

scoringAnchors.example: The text tells us three things about Mary's arrival: first, that Mrs Medlock collected her from the station; second, that it was raining and dark when they arrived; and third, that the house had 'about a hundred rooms, most of them shut up and locked'.
scoringAnchors.rationale: Retrieves multiple distinct details, numbers them, and includes a direct quote — organised and specific

scoringAnchors.example: In the first three pages alone, Burnett gives us five key facts about Mary: she was born in India, her parents neglected her, the servants called her 'Mary Quite Contrary', she had a 'thin angry face', and 'she had never had a plaything she had not wanted'. Each of these sets up a character detail that becomes important later.
scoringAnchors.rationale: Exhaustive retrieval across a defined section, precise references, and recognition that retrieved details serve a narrative purpose

promptGuidance.whenHigh: Strong retrieval. Reduce locate-and-quote questions — push toward inference and evaluation: 'Now that you've found what it says, what do you think it means?'
promptGuidance.whenLow: Weak retrieval. Direct the student back to the text: 'Can you find the part where it tells us about X?' Use closed questions to build scanning confidence.
promptGuidance.whenMedium: Partial retrieval. Prompt for completeness: 'You found one detail — can you find any others?' and for specificity: 'Can you find the exact words it uses?'

parameters.name: inference_skill
parameters.description: Ability to draw conclusions not explicitly stated in the text and justify them with evidence

ks2.name: Make inferences from the text / explain and justify inferences with evidence from the text

subMetrics.name: Beyond Literal
subMetrics.description: Goes beyond what is explicitly written

definitions.high: Identifies implied meaning, subtext, tone, and mood
definitions.low: Only restates what is directly written

subMetrics.name: Justification Quality
subMetrics.description: How well the student explains and defends their inference

definitions.high: Explains reasoning clearly — 'I think X because the text says Y, which suggests Z'
definitions.low: Makes claims without explaining the reasoning behind them

subMetrics.name: Textual Grounding
subMetrics.description: Connects inferences back to specific text

definitions.high: Points to specific words, phrases, or passages that support the inference

interpretationScale.label: No Evidence
interpretationScale.implication: Only repeats what is explicitly written

interpretationScale.label: Emerging
interpretationScale.implication: Attempts inference but stays close to literal text

interpretationScale.label: Developing
interpretationScale.implication: Draws some valid inferences but justification is incomplete

interpretationScale.label: Secure
interpretationScale.implication: Consistent inference with textual support and clear justification

interpretationScale.label: Mastery
interpretationScale.implication: Sophisticated inference connecting subtext, tone, and implication with rigorous justification

scoringAnchors.example: It says she went to the garden. So she went to the garden.
scoringAnchors.rationale: Pure literal restatement, no inference at all

scoringAnchors.example: I think she was sad? Because it says she was alone.
scoringAnchors.rationale: Attempts inference (sad) but stays very close to literal (alone = sad), tentative, minimal justification

scoringAnchors.example: When it says 'she looked at the key as if it were a living thing', I think that means the garden is becoming really important to her — like it's giving her purpose.
scoringAnchors.rationale: Valid inference from figurative language, connects to character motivation, references specific text

scoringAnchors.example: The writer uses the word 'contrary' repeatedly — I don't think it's just describing Mary's behaviour. It's almost like the narrator is challenging us to look past our first impression of her. I think this because the word appears right before moments where Mary does something surprisingly kind, which makes us question the label.
scoringAnchors.rationale: Infers authorial technique from word choice patterns, meta-level reading, and justifies with pattern evidence

scoringAnchors.example: The shift from passive to active voice when Mary starts working in the garden — 'things were done for her' becomes 'she dug and pulled' — mirrors her psychological transformation. Burnett is using grammar itself as a metaphor for agency. I can justify this because every passage set in India uses passive constructions while the garden scenes use active ones — it's too consistent to be accidental.
scoringAnchors.rationale: Linguistic inference at structural level, connects form to meaning, and provides systematic justification across multiple text passages

promptGuidance.whenHigh: Student infers well. Push toward evaluative inference: 'Why do you think the writer chose to do it this way?' or 'Could it mean something different?'
promptGuidance.whenLow: Student stays literal. Model inference explicitly: 'When it says X, it might suggest Y. What do you think?' Use the text to scaffold.
promptGuidance.whenMedium: Student attempts inference but needs grounding. Ask 'What in the text makes you think that?' to build the justify-with-evidence habit.

parameters.name: vocabulary_in_context
parameters.description: Ability to determine the meaning of words and phrases from context, including figurative language

ks2.name: Give / explain the meaning of words in context

subMetrics.name: Context Use
subMetrics.description: Uses surrounding text to work out meaning rather than guessing

definitions.high: Explains how surrounding sentences or the wider passage helped determine meaning
definitions.low: Guesses at meaning without reference to surrounding text

subMetrics.name: Precision
subMetrics.description: How accurately the student defines or explains the word

definitions.high: Gives an accurate definition or synonym that fits the specific context
definitions.low: Gives a vague, wrong, or context-inappropriate definition

subMetrics.name: Figurative Awareness
subMetrics.description: Recognises when language is used non-literally

definitions.high: Identifies metaphors, similes, idioms, and irony — explains both literal and figurative meaning
definitions.low: Takes all language literally, misses figurative uses

interpretationScale.label: No Evidence
interpretationScale.implication: Cannot explain word meanings, skips unfamiliar words

interpretationScale.label: Emerging
interpretationScale.implication: Attempts definitions but often inaccurate or context-free

interpretationScale.label: Developing
interpretationScale.implication: Can explain common words in context but struggles with figurative language

interpretationScale.label: Secure
interpretationScale.implication: Accurately explains words using context clues, recognises figurative uses

interpretationScale.label: Mastery
interpretationScale.implication: Explains nuanced and figurative language with precision, distinguishes multiple meanings

scoringAnchors.example: I don't know what 'contrary' means. I skipped that bit.
scoringAnchors.rationale: No attempt to use context, avoids unfamiliar vocabulary entirely

scoringAnchors.example: 'Contrary' means... like, bad? She's a bad person?
scoringAnchors.rationale: Vague approximation without using context clues, confuses personality trait with moral judgement

scoringAnchors.example: 'Contrary' means difficult or awkward — like she does the opposite of what people want. The bit where it says nobody liked her makes me think it means she's hard to get along with.
scoringAnchors.rationale: Accurate definition in context, references surrounding text to support meaning, but stays at surface level

scoringAnchors.example: 'Contrary' here means stubbornly doing the opposite — but it's also a reference to the nursery rhyme 'Mary, Mary, Quite Contrary'. The servants actually use it as a nickname. So it works on two levels: her personality AND a literary reference that the reader would recognise.
scoringAnchors.rationale: Precise definition, identifies the intertextual reference, explains dual meaning, uses evidence from the text

scoringAnchors.example: 'Contrary' is doing a lot of work in this text. Literally it means perverse or wilful — she resists everything. But Burnett also uses it structurally: Mary is 'contrary' to what we expect of a heroine. She's ugly, rude, and unloved. The word sets up the entire transformation arc because by the end, she's the opposite of contrary — she's nurturing. So the meaning of the word shifts as the character does.
scoringAnchors.rationale: Multi-layered analysis: literal meaning, structural function, and semantic shift across the narrative — demonstrates how vocabulary serves authorial purpose

promptGuidance.whenHigh: Strong vocabulary skills. Introduce more complex words and figurative language: 'What tone does that word create?' or 'Could the author have chosen a different word? What would change?'
promptGuidance.whenLow: Weak vocabulary. Pause at unfamiliar words: 'Let's look at the words around it — what clues do they give us about what it might mean?' Model the context-use strategy.
promptGuidance.whenMedium: Developing vocabulary. Push beyond basic definitions: 'You said it means X — but why did the author choose that particular word instead of just saying X?'

parameters.name: language_appreciation
parameters.description: Ability to identify and explain how an author's choice of words and phrases enhances meaning

pirls.name: Evaluate and Critique Content and Textual Elements

ks2.name: Identify / explain how meaning is enhanced through choice of words and phrases

subMetrics.name: Technique Identification
subMetrics.description: Spots specific language techniques used by the author

definitions.high: Identifies simile, metaphor, personification, repetition, contrast, etc. by name or description
definitions.low: Cannot identify any language techniques, reads text at face value

subMetrics.name: Effect Explanation
subMetrics.description: Explains what effect the technique has on the reader

definitions.high: Articulates the emotional, visual, or intellectual impact of the language choice
definitions.low: Identifies a technique but cannot explain why it matters

subMetrics.name: Authorial Intent
subMetrics.description: Connects language choices to the author's purpose

definitions.high: Explains why the author made this specific choice — what they wanted the reader to think or feel
definitions.low: Treats language choices as accidental or decorative

interpretationScale.label: No Evidence
interpretationScale.implication: No awareness of language as a deliberate choice

interpretationScale.label: Emerging
interpretationScale.implication: Notices some language features but cannot explain their effect

interpretationScale.label: Developing
interpretationScale.implication: Identifies techniques and attempts to explain effect, but analysis is thin

interpretationScale.label: Secure
interpretationScale.implication: Consistently identifies techniques, explains effects, and connects to authorial purpose

interpretationScale.label: Mastery
interpretationScale.implication: Sophisticated analysis of how language creates meaning at multiple levels

scoringAnchors.example: It just describes her. I don't know why the author wrote it like that.
scoringAnchors.rationale: No awareness that language choices are deliberate or meaningful

scoringAnchors.example: There's a simile here — 'as tyrannical and selfish a little pig as ever lived'. It compares her to a pig.
scoringAnchors.rationale: Identifies the technique (simile) but stops there — no explanation of effect or purpose

scoringAnchors.example: Calling her 'a little pig' is a simile that makes her sound disgusting. It's a really harsh comparison — pigs are dirty and greedy, so it makes the reader dislike her straight away.
scoringAnchors.rationale: Identifies technique, explains the effect on the reader, but doesn't connect to the author's wider purpose

scoringAnchors.example: Burnett piles up negative language in one sentence — 'tyrannical', 'selfish', 'pig'. The accumulation is deliberate: she wants us to feel repelled by Mary at the start so that the transformation later is more powerful. If Mary started as likeable, the garden's effect wouldn't mean as much.
scoringAnchors.rationale: Identifies accumulation technique, explains reader effect, and connects to authorial strategy across the wider narrative

scoringAnchors.example: Notice the register shift between the India chapters and the garden chapters. In India, Burnett uses Latinate, formal vocabulary — 'tyrannical', 'imperious', 'languid'. In Yorkshire, the language becomes Anglo-Saxon and earthy — 'dig', 'soil', 'grow', 'fresh'. The language itself enacts Mary's transformation: she moves from an artificial, colonial register to something natural and rooted. Burnett is using the sound and origin of the words themselves to carry meaning.
scoringAnchors.rationale: Analysis at the level of register and etymology — identifies a pattern across the whole text, connects linguistic form to thematic meaning, demonstrates sophisticated awareness of how language creates meaning beyond individual techniques

promptGuidance.whenHigh: Strong language appreciation. Push toward cross-text comparison: 'How does this author's language compare to the other text we read?' or 'Could a different technique achieve the same effect?'
promptGuidance.whenLow: No language awareness. Start with accessible techniques: 'The author could have said "a bad girl" but instead said "a little pig". Why might they have chosen those words?' Make the choice visible.
promptGuidance.whenMedium: Identifies techniques but analysis is thin. Push for effect: 'You spotted the simile — now tell me: what does it make you feel as a reader? Why did the author want you to feel that?'

parameters.name: evaluation_skill
parameters.description: Ability to interpret, integrate, and evaluate information across a text — forming and supporting judgements about content, structure, and meaning

pirls.name: Interpret and Integrate Ideas and Information / Evaluate and Critique

ks2.name: Identify / explain how information / narrative content contributes to meaning as a whole + make comparisons within the text

subMetrics.name: Interpretation Depth
subMetrics.description: How far beyond the surface the student goes in interpreting themes, messages, and meaning

definitions.high: Identifies and articulates themes, connects parts to the whole, sees how structure creates meaning
definitions.low: Stays at plot summary level, cannot identify broader themes or messages

subMetrics.name: Evidence Integration
subMetrics.description: How well the student weaves textual evidence into their interpretive arguments

definitions.high: Evidence is embedded naturally in reasoning — 'The phrase X shows that Y because Z'
definitions.low: Makes claims without evidence, or quotes without connecting to the argument

subMetrics.name: Critical Judgement
subMetrics.description: Forms and defends an evaluative position about the text

definitions.high: Makes comparisons, considers alternatives, evaluates effectiveness of authorial choices
definitions.low: Accepts the text passively, makes no evaluative statements

interpretationScale.label: No Evidence
interpretationScale.implication: Cannot move beyond plot summary or literal content

interpretationScale.label: Emerging
interpretationScale.implication: Identifies a theme or message but cannot develop or support it

interpretationScale.label: Developing
interpretationScale.implication: Interprets with some evidence but analysis lacks depth or critical perspective

interpretationScale.label: Secure
interpretationScale.implication: Integrates evidence into coherent interpretation, makes some evaluative judgements

interpretationScale.label: Mastery
interpretationScale.implication: Sophisticated multi-layered interpretation with seamless evidence integration and critical evaluation

scoringAnchors.example: It's about a girl who finds a garden. She makes friends and the garden grows.
scoringAnchors.rationale: Pure plot summary, no interpretation of meaning or theme

scoringAnchors.example: I think it's about being lonely. The girl is lonely because her parents died.
scoringAnchors.rationale: Identifies a valid theme (loneliness) but stays at surface level, no evidence, no development

scoringAnchors.example: The story is about transformation — Mary starts as this spoiled, neglected child and the garden kind of changes her. Like she grows as a person while the garden grows. The bit where she first sees the robin seems important because that's when she starts caring about something.
scoringAnchors.rationale: Identifies transformation theme, begins to connect parts (robin scene) to the whole (character arc), uses some evidence but analysis is still developing

scoringAnchors.example: I think the garden represents renewal — not just for Mary but for everyone who touches it. Colin's healing mirrors the garden's restoration, which is evidence that Burnett is using the garden as a symbol rather than just a setting. It's about how nurturing something outside yourself can heal what's broken inside. But I wonder if the story oversimplifies this — can nature really 'cure' Colin's illness? The text seems to suggest it can, which feels like wish-fulfilment rather than realism.
scoringAnchors.rationale: Multi-layered thematic insight, connects symbolism across characters, integrates evidence, and makes a critical judgement about the text's credibility

scoringAnchors.example: What strikes me is how Burnett uses the garden as a counterpoint to the colonial setting Mary comes from — the neglected English garden versus the 'managed' world of India. The theme isn't just renewal, it's about what happens when you stop trying to control nature and let things grow wild. That applies to the children too — they thrive when freed from adult control. Comparing the early India chapters with the garden chapters, you can see this structurally: every India scene has adults giving orders while every garden scene has children making decisions. The text is arguing that freedom, not management, produces growth.
scoringAnchors.rationale: Sophisticated multi-layered analysis connecting themes across the whole text structure, integrates comparative evidence from multiple sections, forms an original critical argument about what the text is 'arguing', demonstrates evaluation of authorial choices at the structural level

promptGuidance.whenHigh: Student evaluates well. Challenge with competing interpretations: 'That's one reading — but what if someone argued the opposite? What evidence would they use?' Push toward comparing within and across texts.
promptGuidance.whenLow: Student stays at plot level. Scaffold toward theme: 'What is this story really about — not what happens, but what it means?' Use closed questions first if needed.
promptGuidance.whenMedium: Student interprets but doesn't integrate evidence or evaluate. Ask: 'Where in the text do you see that?' and 'Do you think the author does this effectively? Why or why not?'

parameters.name: recall_accuracy
parameters.description: Ability to remember and reference content from prior sessions — retention and consolidation

hf.rationale: Cross-session retention — neither KS2 nor PIRLS assess this as they assume single-sitting tests. HF measures retention across sessions as a learning consolidation signal.

subMetrics.name: Content Accuracy
subMetrics.description: Whether recalled content is factually correct

definitions.high: Recalls content accurately without confusion or invention
definitions.low: Confuses details, invents content, or recalls nothing

definitions.high: Recalls specific details, quotes, character names, plot points
definitions.low: Only vague impressions ('it was about a girl')

subMetrics.name: Spontaneity
subMetrics.description: Whether recall is spontaneous or requires heavy prompting

definitions.high: Recalls without prompting or with minimal cues
definitions.low: Cannot recall even with specific prompts

interpretationScale.label: No Evidence
interpretationScale.implication: No recall of prior session content

interpretationScale.label: Emerging
interpretationScale.implication: Vague recall with significant prompting needed

interpretationScale.label: Developing
interpretationScale.implication: Recalls main ideas but misses details

interpretationScale.label: Secure
interpretationScale.implication: Accurate recall of themes and key details

interpretationScale.label: Mastery
interpretationScale.implication: Detailed, spontaneous recall connecting across sessions

scoringAnchors.example: I don't remember. We talked about something... a book maybe?
scoringAnchors.rationale: Essentially no recall of prior session content

scoringAnchors.example: Oh yeah, we read about... a girl and a garden? I can't remember her name though.
scoringAnchors.rationale: Vague plot recall, no character names or theme recollection

scoringAnchors.example: Last time we talked about Mary and how she found the garden. We said the garden was like a symbol for something... growth maybe?
scoringAnchors.rationale: Recalls character name, basic plot, and begins to recall thematic discussion

scoringAnchors.example: We discussed how Mary transforms from this spoiled child into someone who cares about the garden and other people. You asked me about the word 'contrary' and I said I thought the narrator was being ironic.
scoringAnchors.rationale: Accurate recall of discussion content, specific vocabulary, and own contributions

scoringAnchors.example: In session 1 we covered the opening chapters — Mary's isolation in India and her neglect. In session 2 we looked at the garden discovery and I argued that the garden represents Mary's inner world. Today I want to revisit that because I've been thinking about whether Colin's garden experience supports or complicates that reading.
scoringAnchors.rationale: Detailed cross-session recall, recalls own interpretations, builds on prior learning, self-directed

promptGuidance.whenHigh: Strong recall. Build on it: 'You mentioned X last time — has your thinking changed?' Use their memory as a foundation for deeper work.
promptGuidance.whenLow: Weak recall. Use structured retrieval: 'Last time we talked about [topic]. Can you remember anything about that?' Provide cues rather than answers.
promptGuidance.whenMedium: Partial recall. Use spaced retrieval technique: 'What was the main theme we identified?' If they struggle, narrow it: 'Was it about loneliness or transformation?'
